seoTitle: How to Do Backlog Grooming in Jira
metaDescription: Run an effective backlog grooming session in Jira by reviewing, refining, and reprioritizing issues before they hit a sprint.
intro: An unrefined backlog leads to confused sprint planning and half-written stories. Backlog grooming in Jira is about regularly reviewing that list so only clear, prioritized work makes it into upcoming sprints. Here's how to approach it.

steps.heading: Open the backlog view
steps.body: Go to your Scrum project's backlog to see all unscheduled issues waiting to be worked on.

steps.heading: Review and clean up old issues
steps.body: Close or update issues that are outdated, duplicated, or no longer relevant to the current project direction.

steps.heading: Add missing detail to unclear issues
steps.body: Flesh out thin descriptions, acceptance criteria, or estimates on issues that are too vague to work on yet.

steps.heading: Reprioritize the list
steps.body: Drag issues up or down based on current priorities so the top of the backlog reflects what should be tackled next.

steps.heading: Prepare top issues for the next sprint
steps.body: Make sure the highest-priority issues are estimated and ready to be pulled into sprint planning.

faq.question: How often should backlog grooming happen?
faq.answer: Most teams groom the backlog weekly or biweekly, often just before sprint planning.

faq.question: Who should attend a grooming session?
faq.answer: Typically the product owner and a few key team members, rather than the entire development team.

faq.question: What's the goal of grooming versus sprint planning?
faq.answer: Grooming refines and prioritizes the backlog ahead of time, while sprint planning selects the actual work for the upcoming sprint.

faq.question: Should every backlog issue be estimated during grooming?
faq.answer: Only the issues near the top that are likely to be picked up soon really need estimates during grooming.
